The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Government Budget and the Economy is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 4Chapter 4 Determination of Income and Determination of Income and Determination of Income and Determination of Income and Determination of Income and Employment Employment Employment Employment Employment We have so far talked about the national income, price level, rate of interest etc. in an ad hoc manner – without investigating the forces that govern their values.

The text explains that the basic objective of macroeconomics is to develop theoretical tools, called models, capable of describing the processes which determine the values of these variables.

The text explains that specifically, the models attempt to provide theoretical explanation to questions such as what causes periods of slow growth or recessions in the economy, or increment in the price level, or a rise in unemployment.

The text explains that it is difficult to account for all the variables at the same time.

The text explains that thus, when we concentrate on the determination of a particular variable, we must hold the values of all other variables constant.

The text explains that this is a stylisation typical of almost any theoretical exercise and is called the assumption of ceteris paribus, which literally means ‘other things remaining equal’.

The text explains that you can think of the procedure as follows – in order to solve for the values of two variables x and y from two equations, we solve for one variable, say x, in terms of y from one equation first, and then substitute this value into the other equation to obtain the complete solution.

The text explains that we apply the same method in the analysis of the macroeconomic system.

The text explains that in this chapter we deal with the determination of National Income under the assumption of fixed price of final goods and constant rate of interest in the economy.

The text explains that the theoretical model used in this chapter is based on the theory given by John Maynard Keynes.

The text explains that 4.1 AGGREGATE DEMAND AND ITS COMPONENTS In the chapter on National Income Accounting, we have come across terms like consumption, investment, or the total output of final goods and services in an economy (GDP).

The text explains that in Chapter 2 they were used in the accounting sense – denoting actual values of these items as measured by the activities within the economy in a certain year.

The text explains that we call these actual or accounting values ex post measures of these items.

The text explains that these terms, however, can be used with a different connotation.

The text explains that consumption may denote not what people have actually consumed in a given year, but what they

The text explains that 54 54 54 54 54 Introductory Macroeconomics had planned to consume during the same period.

The text explains that it may be explained as a rate of change of consumption as income changes.

The text explains that investment Investment is defined as addition to the stock of physical capital (such as machines, buildings, roads etc., i.e. anything that adds to the future productive capacity of the economy) and changes in the inventory (or the stock of finished goods) of a producer.

The text explains that machines produced in an economy in a given year are not ‘used up’ to produce other goods but yield their services over a number of years.

The text explains that we can write the ex ante investment demand as I = I (4.2) where I is a positive constant which represents the autonomous (given or exogenous) investment in the economy in a given year.

The text explains that 4.2 DETERMINATION OF INCOME IN TWO-SECTOR MODEL In an economy without a government, the ex ante aggregate demand for final goods is the sum total of the ex ante consumption expenditure and ex ante investment expenditure on such goods, viz.

The text explains that this equation can be further simplified by adding up the two autonomous terms, C and I , making it Y = A + c.Y (4.3) where A = C + I is the total autonomous expenditure in the economy.

The text explains that c , representing subsistence consumption level of an economy, remains more or less stable over time.

The text explains that on the other hand, the expression on the right hand side denotes ex ante or planned aggregate demand for final goods in the economy.

The text explains that ex ante supply is equal to ex ante demand only when the final goods market, and hence the economy, is in equilibrium.

The text explains that equation (4.3) should not, therefore, be confused with the accounting identity of Chapter 2, which states that the ex post value of total output must always be equal to the sum total of ex post consumption and ex post investment in the economy.

The text explains that the inventory investment can take place due to two reasons: (i) the firm decides to keep some stocks for various reasons (this is called planned inventory investment) (ii) the sales differ from the planned level of sales, in which case the firm has to add to/run down existing inventories (this is called unplanned inventory investment).

The text explains that thus even though planned Thus even though planned Thus even though planned Thus even though planned Thus even though planned Y YY YY is is is is is greater than planned greater than planned greater than planned greater than planned greater than planned C C C C C +++++ IIIII, actual , actual , actual , actual , actual Y YY YY will be equal to actual will be equal to actual will be equal to actual will be equal to actual will be equal to actual C C C C C +++++ IIIII, with , with , with , with , with the extra output showing up as unintended accumulation of inventories the extra output showing up as unintended accumulation of inventories the extra output showing up as unintended accumulation of inventories the extra output showing up as unintended accumulation of inventories the extra output showing up as unintended accumulation of inventories in the ex post in the ex post in the ex post in the ex post in the ex post IIIII on the right hand side of the accounting identity on the right hand side of the accounting identity on the right hand side of the accounting identity on the right hand side of the accounting identity on the right hand side of the accounting identity.

The text explains that at this point, we can introduce a government in this economy.

The text explains that the major economic activities of the government that affect the aggregate demand for final goods and services can be summarized by the fiscal variables Tax (T) and Government Expenditure (G), both autonomous to our analysis.

The text explains that government, through its expenditure G on final goods and services, adds to the aggregate demand like other firms and households.

The text explains that on the other hand, taxes imposed by the government take a part of the income away from the household, whose disposable income, therefore, becomes Yd = Y – T.

The text explains that hence, equation (4.3) has to be modified in the following way to incorporate the government Y = C + I + G + c (Y – T ) Note that G – c.T , like C or I , just adds to the autonomous term A .

The text explains that we shall, for the sake of simplicity, ignore the government sector for the rest of this chapter.

The text explains that observe also, that without the government imposing indirect taxes and subsidies, the total value of final goods and services produced in the economy, GDP, becomes identically equal to the National Income.

The text explains that henceforth, throughout the rest of the chapter, we shall refer to Y as GDP or National Income interchangeably.

The text explains that two reasons can be put forward: (i) at the first stage, we are assuming an economy with unused resources: machineries, buildings and labours.

The text explains that 4.3.1 Macroeconomic Equilibrium with Price Level Fixed 4.3.1 Macroeconomic Equilibrium with Price Level Fixed 4.3.1 Macroeconomic Equilibrium with Price Level Fixed 4.3.1 Macroeconomic Equilibrium with Price Level Fixed 4.3.1 Macroeconomic Equilibrium with Price Level Fixed (A) (A) (A) (A) (A) Graphical Method Graphical Method Graphical Method Graphical Method Graphical Method As already explained, the consumers demand can be expressed by the equation C C cY  

The text explains that to answer this question we will need to recall the “intercept form of the linear equation”, Y = a + bX Here, the variables are X and Y and there is a linear relation between them. a and b are constants.

The text explains that consumption Function – Graphical Representation Using the same logic, the consumption function can be shown as follows: Consumption function, where, C = intercept of the consumption function c = slope of consumption function = tan α Investment Function – Graphical Representation In a two sector model, there are two sources of final demand, the first is consumption and the second is investment.

The text explains that here, aggregate supply or the GDP is assumed to smoothly move up or down since they are unused resources of all types available.

The text explains that now, the 45° line has the feature that every point on it has the same horizontal and vertical coordinates.

The text explains that (B) Algebraic Method Ex ante aggregate demand = I C cY + + Ex ante aggregate supply = Y Equilibrium requires that the plans of suppliers are matched by plans of those who provide final demands in the economy.

The text explains that e1F measures the amount of excess demand that emerges in the economy as a result of the increase in autonomous expenditure.

The text explains that summary Summary Summary Summary Summary K K K K Key Conceptsey Concepts ey Concepts ey Concepts ey Concepts Aggregate demand Aggregate supply Equilibrium Ex ante Ex post Ex ante consumption Marginal propensity to consume Ex ante investment Unintended changes in inventories Autonomous change Parametric shift Effective demand principle Paradox of thrift Autonomous expenditure multiplier 1.

The text explains that how is it related to marginal propensity to save?

The text explains that what is the difference between ex ante investment and ex post investment?

The text explains that what do you understand by ‘parametric shift of a line’?

The text explains that how does a line shift when its (i) slope decreases, and (ii) its intercept increases?

The text explains that how will you derive the autonomous expenditure multiplier when price of final goods and the rate of interest are given?

The text explains that measure the level of ex-ante aggregate demand when autonomous investment and consumption expenditure (A) is Rs 50 crores, and MPS is 0.2 and level of income (Y) is Rs 4000 crores.

The text explains that state whether the economy is in equilibrium or not (cite reasons).

Source: NCERT Class 12 Introductory Macroeconomics, Chapter 4: Government Budget and the Economy
